title: Nicotine-related beliefs induce dose-dependent responses in a thalamic circuit in human smokers
core_claim: Instructed beliefs about nicotine strength in nicotine-dependent humans modulate value-related thalamic activity and thalamus-vmPFC functional coupling in a dose-dependent manner, while actual nicotine intake, cotinine metabolism and striatal reward responses are unchanged. This demonstrates that subjective beliefs can map onto neural substrates in a manner quantitatively analogous to pharmacological dose-response curves.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.purpose: Motivate the problem (how beliefs map onto neurobiology in addiction), introduce dose-response reasoning, state hypothesis that belief can drive dose-like neural responses via thalamic nAChR-rich circuits, preview design (20 nicotine-dependent smokers, 3 within-subject belief conditions over 60 scans, 31 non-smoking healthy controls).
key_points: Beliefs shape behavior and wellbeing but precise mapping to neural substrates is unknown
Placebo and belief-drug interaction literature show beliefs affect neural responses but in binary all-or-none manner
Pharmacology relies on dose-response to establish precision; whether beliefs can do the same is an open question
Thalamus is dense in nAChRs and nicotine drives dose-responses there; nAChR occupancy can occur at low doses or from second-hand smoke, implying cognitive/belief factors
Hypothesis: instructed beliefs about nicotine strength will drive parametric, dose-like neural responses in thalamic and thalamo-cortical circuitry
Study overview: e-cigarette with constant 1.2% nicotine, labelled low/medium/high across three visits, decision-making fMRI

sections.title: Results
sections.purpose: Present belief manipulation check, thalamic dose-response, control for sensorimotor confounds, negative result in striatum, thalamus-vmPFC PPI result.
key_points: Manipulation check: subjective perceived nicotine strength scales with instructed dose (rmANOVA F(2,38)=9.71, P=0.0004, partial eta^2=0.34)
Sanity: nicotine intake (P=0.178), cotinine (P=0.393), CO (P=0.698) do not differ across belief conditions
Whole-brain GLM: thalamic value-tracking signals scale parametrically with belief (cluster PFWE=0.006, k=50, peak MNI -15,-19,-1)
Thalamic ROI: rmANOVA F(2,38)=3.62, P=0.036, partial eta^2=0.16; not different from healthy controls
Permutation test on thalamic betas (N=1000, P=0.002)
Multivariate decoding of belief from thalamic voxel patterns at 49.3% accuracy vs chance 33.3% (surrogate 33.1 +/- 6.3%, P=0.011); VPL survives FDR (P=0.018)
Subjective perceived strength correlates with thalamic BOLD across participants (Spearman r=0.27, P=0.035)
Visual and motor regressors and no-smoothing pipeline replicate the effect (rule out sensorimotor)
Nucleus accumbens tracks value overall but does not distinguish belief conditions (rmANOVA F(2,38)=0.056, P=0.945; decoding 34.0% vs chance 33.3%, surrogate 32.1 +/- 6.3%, P=0.372)
Putamen (F(2,38)=1.15, P=0.327) and caudate (F(2,38)=0.24, P=0.781) also null
PPI: thalamus-vmPFC functional coupling scales with belief (whole-brain P=0.041; ROI based on Rouault and Fleming vmPFC sphere)

sections.title: Discussion
sections.purpose: Interpret thalamic dose-response, discuss vmPFC-thalamus circuit, address divergence from prior striatum finding, consider limits, outline implications for mental health and placebo research.
key_points: Thalamus as precise belief substrate in nicotine addiction
vmPFC-thalamus coupling encodes parametric belief dosage, not binary switch
Reconciling striatum null here vs Gu et al. 2015: e-cig novelty vs conditioned cigarette cues; higher nicotine intake (~0.8 mg vs ~0.6 mg)
nAChR density biology supports thalamus locus
Limitations: modest smoker sample (n=20), single decision-making task, control group drawn from separate cohort
Implications: belief as measurable dose-like variable for personalized treatment and placebo design

sections.title: Methods
sections.purpose: Document participants, double-blind instruction, cotinine LC-MS/MS, investment task, fMRI acquisition, GLM, rLDA decoding, PPI.
key_points: 20 nicotine-dependent smokers (6 female, 41.1 +/- 11.97 y); 31 non-smoking HC (15 female, 28 +/- 9 y)
blu e-cigarette with 1.2% nicotine cartridges relabelled as low/medium/high
Cotinine via LC-MS/MS with d3-cotinine IS, SCIEX QTRAP 6500+
Investment task with positive/negative bets, |r_t| as reward magnitude
3T Philips Achieva, TR=2000 ms, TE=25 ms, 3.4x3.4x4 mm voxels, 38 slices, ArtRepair
SPM12 GLM, rmANOVA second level, cluster FWE P<0.05 with cluster-defining P<0.005 and k=50
rLDA (fitdiscr, MATLAB) with 10-fold CV, permutation N=10000
PPI with thalamus seed, vmPFC sphere from Rouault and Fleming 2018

anti_leakage_notes: All numbers and claims come only from experimental_log.md and idea_summary.md. Do not invoke memory for author names, affiliations, or unreported statistics. No tables were extracted from source; Table 1 here is composed only from numbers in the log.
